title: SEGMENT 15 — HSCT for sickle cell: the original cure

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — HSCT for sickle cell: the original cure
Now let's tell the story that brings all of this together.
Sickle cell disease is one of the oldest known molecular diseases — Linus Pauling described its molecular basis in 1949 as the prototype of "molecular disease.
" It is caused by a single-base mutation in the beta-globin gene that produces hemoglobin S, which polymerizes when deoxygenated, deforming red blood cells into sickle shapes.
The sickled cells clog small blood vessels, causing the agonizing vaso-occlusive crises, organ damage, strokes, and premature death that define the disease.
For decades, sickle cell disease had only supportive treatments: transfusions, hydroxyurea, pain management.
Then in the 1980s, transplant pioneers began trying allogeneic HSCT — matched sibling donor transplants for selected pediatric patients — and found that transplant could cure sickle cell.
The donor stem cells engrafted, produced normal (non-sickling) hemoglobin, and the disease was eliminated.
Cure rates approached 85 to 90 percent for matched sibling pediatric transplants.
But the catch was access.
Only about 20 percent of sickle cell patients have an HLA-matched sibling.
The disease is most common in patients of African ancestry, who are dramatically underrepresented in adult donor registries.
So the cure existed, but most patients couldn't access it.
Even those who could face significant GVHD risk and treatment-related mortality of 5 to 10 percent.

The famous case was the Berlin patient — Timothy Brown — who was cured of HIV in 2007 by an allogeneic HSCT from a donor with a CCR5 mutation, and who incidentally was also cured of any underlying blood condition by the transplant.
But the broader picture for sickle cell was that allogeneic transplant was a powerful but limited tool.
What changed everything was the realization that you could, instead of finding a matched donor, edit the patient's own stem cells.
Use autologous HSCT — no donor matching, no GVHD — but engineer the patient's HSCs to overcome the genetic defect.
That's the foundation of the modern gene therapy era for sickle cell, which we'll cover next.

The historical lesson: HSCT was the original cure for sickle cell, it worked, it had access barriers, and those barriers are what gene therapy is solving — by replacing the donor with the patient's own edited cells.
